original_abstract:
Calculating nutrient density based on standard metrics—such as 100 kcal, 100 g/mL, 50 g, or the Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC)—often produces a distorted representation of a food's true nutritional value. Reliance on these metrics for dietary selection can inadvertently increase the risk of chronic disease by promoting foods with poor nutritional profiles. This study evaluated 8,596 food items across 25 categories and 29 food components by comparing the regulatory standards of the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) against a proposed optimized procedure.

Our findings indicate that selecting foods based on current FDA and CAC guidelines frequently results in energy intake exceeding metabolic requirements when attempting to meet targets for beneficial nutrients (such as vitamins, protein, dietary fiber, and minerals). Conversely, many foods categorized as acceptable under CAC guidelines failed to meet essential nutrient demands. Furthermore, food selections based on these standard metrics often led to the overconsumption of "negative" components, including cholesterol, total fat, saturated fat, and sodium. Many nutritionally sound options were paradoxically excluded by existing regulatory frameworks. 

The analysis reveals significant vulnerabilities in current food selection models. When evaluated using the refined scoring system for nutritional quality, the highest-rated categories across all age groups (children over four and adults) were fast foods (evaluated by serving size under CAC/FDA), spices and herbs (per 100 g/mL under CAC), and vegetables (per 100 kcal under CAC). These results underscore the need for more nuanced reference standards to better align dietary choices with actual health outcomes.
humanized_abstract:
The current practice of evaluating nutrient density using reference amounts such as 100 kcal, 100 g/mL, 50 g, or the Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC) does not accurately reflect the nutritional value of the foods we eat. Using these reference amounts for food selection can lead to a higher risk of developing chronic diseases due to the consumption of less nutritious foods. In this study, the nutrient and food composition data for 8596 foods across 25 categories and 29 nutrients were analyzed and compared with the standards as defined by the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regulations and proposed in the optimized procedure.

Using the FDA and CAC food guidelines to select the food items of each serving portion led to an energy intake above the estimated energy requirements for achieving the specified levels of essential vitamins, protein, dietary fiber and minerals. In addition, several of the foods listed in the official CAC guidelines did not adequately cover the required nutrient needs. Moreover, the official guidelines, as well as general criteria currently used, have led to the overconsumption of the so-called “negative” nutrients including cholesterol, total fat, saturated fat and sodium. Many of the nutritionally balanced foods, which generally appear to meet current official criteria for the allowed foods, were excluded by these standards.

According to the study, current models used to select foods have revealed many flaws. Applying the new score for evaluating the nutritional quality of food per serving (CAC/FDA) for children aged 4 and over, and for adults, resulted in fast foods belonging to the top scoring category in all cases, spices and herbs per 100 g/mL under CAC, and vegetables per 100 kcal under CAC for children and adults. So new and more accurate reference points are required in order to achieve an optimum and balanced diet for everyone, capable of promoting real health gains.
